Those exact chain of events was what got them where they were right then - dealing with a panicked Chaerin who was horrified her sister was going to break if she found out the truth. "I know she's going to ask! What am I gonna do? Oh my God..." she's muttering to herself now, burying her face in her hands as she paces back and forth around the living room.

None of them had ever seen the younger sister so nervous before. It had them exchanging glances, trying to figure out what to say in response.

"It's fine, Chaerin," Chanhee advises, "Solar is going to have to face it one of these days, either way. We can't keep her in the dark forever."

"We could," Hyunjoon snarkily remarks, crossing one leg over the other and taking a hit of his cigarette, blowing it into the air before he continues, "she was doing just fine when I was supplying her."

Chanhee shot the boy a glare. "Oh sure, as she was literally killing herself, right!"

Hyunjoon rolled his eyes, "oh, honey, how do you manage to be more dramatic than me?"

"Look, Solar already knew his condition wasn't getting better," Sangyeon sighs, tired of the endless bickering and useless conversation, "it's not like we're giving up on him, and it's not like he's given up yet, remember? We are taking any chance there is that he'll wake up."

Chaerin finally stopped pacing, shaking her head in dismay as she fell onto one of the couches. "Nah, there's just no way," she exhaled weakly, "you guys saw him, right? He doesn't even look alive anymore."

It hurt everyone to hear those words out loud. Of course, they all knew it. They had all gone to visit Sunwoo themselves in pairs after Chaerin told them the news, and they still did go see him everyday. But they never spoke words like that out loud. They would never give up on their leader, never admit that he was losing the battle. Hearing her say it, it was an arrow to their hearts. 

No one said anything, as if they were all sitting there, trying to take it in. The thing was, none of them ever showed any emotion, either. 

"Are you talking about Sunwoo?"

Chaerin looked like she'd seen a ghost at the sight of Solar suddenly standing there. Her green eyes were wide with horror, and she coughed a bit, clearing her throat before she muttered out, "Sol..."

Solar stood there, brows knitted with confusion, eyes darting around everyone in the room. A concerned Jinhwan and Jungkook stood behind her on either side, not seeming to know what to make of the situation. The brunette stared at everyone who was looking at her dumbfounded, seeming to realize that they had been keeping some serious news from her. 

"What are you all talking about?" She snapped, desperate for the truth she knew she hadn't gotten yet, "What do you mean Sunwoo doesn't look alive anymore? What's going on with him?!" When no one answered after a split second, she turned to look at the two boys behind her, "Did you guys know anything about this?!" she demanded, even though it was obvious Jinhwan would have no idea since he hadn't been around. More so, she was focused on Jungkook. 

The doe eyed boy shook his head, "course not, Sol."

Whipping back around, her green eyes were fueled with anger - anger because she felt betrayed by the fact her friends would keep Sunwoo's condition from her. Though, a part of her just felt guilty, guilty because she had forgotten about him for so long due to the drugs, guilty because she could've known what was happening to him herself if she would just go visit him. 

"Listen, Solar, it's not something you need to hear right now," Jaehyun steps up, walking towards her with a soft look on his rough face, "everyone was waiting for the right time to tell you."

"There isn't a right time to tell me!" she cries, not understanding no matter how much they explained. "I can't - I have to go see him." She finally asserts, earning shocked expressions from some of them. Spinning on her heel, she disappeared into the kitchen to grab the keys to the vehicle her brother had rented.

Not seeming to know much about what to do, Jungkook and Jinhwan both followed her, wanting to support her in anyway they could. 

"Solar!" Chaerin had yelled, finally growing the courage to get up from her seat. She rushes over to the girl that was already opening the door, grabbing her arm and pulling her back, "You really don't need to go there right now."

Solar closed her eyes to take in her emotion for a second, before glaring back to her sister, "Chaerin, I have to see him. You don't understand."

"I know I don't, Sol, but I can at least understand how hard it is for you to lose someone like that," she snaps, "so hard that I'm trying to help you so you don't have to see what kind of state he's in... you don't want to see it, Solar."

She was crying.

She didn't know she was until her sister had reached up to wipe the tears that were streaming down her face. Solar's breath got caught in her throat, and she coughed a bit, until her coughs turned into a throaty sob. Chaerin pulled her in for a hug, squeezing her big sister like it was the last time she ever would. 

"I'm sorry, sister," she whispered, "I never wanted this to happen, either."

But it had.

And Sunwoo was dead, whether any of them wanted to say it or not.

"I have to see him..." she whispers, just loud enough for everyone to hear. Sniffling, she turned away, preparing to walk out the door. "I have to..."

"Solar, Chaerin is right," Jaehyun spoke up, walking forward to close the door she was about to walk out of. "Sunwoo is not in a condition you need to see him in, and that is that."

The brunette stares up at her big brother, the only one who was strong enough to tell her no to her face. Her green eyes narrow the more he stares back at her with that strict, mean look in his eyes. She kept thinking he would change his mind, understand that she deserved to see Sunwoo because it could be for the last time. That was, if he was even still there. For all she knew, they could've already had him sent away - cremated. How would she know?

And she had a feeling no one here was going to tell her the full truth. She would just have to find out herself. 

"Jae..." she mumbles, her voice low but full of annoyance. It was said with warning, warning that her big brother was ignoring.

But her big brother had no intention of moving. It fueled Solar with rage, she was in disbelief how they all had the audacity to keep the truth from her, and now they wanted to keep her from even seeing him? It was setting her off. Especially with the drugs coursing through her veins, it was not the time to test her and her patience. 

"I'm going to see him," she snaps matter-of-factly, "so I suggest you move aside."

"Little sister, for the sake of your health, you need to wait," her brother shakes his head, talking in that knowing tone he always liked to use. 

As much as Jaehyun believed he was always right - he wasn't. 

"Just let her go, Jae," Chae calls from behind him with a sigh, "she deserves to see him."

"I agree, that's her boo, you know?" Hyunjoon hums from where he was sitting, "not right to keep him away from her. It's what Sunwoo would want."

The way they talked about him in past tense made her feel sick to her stomach. She still couldn't process it, she kept telling herself all this time that Sunwoo was coming back. Even when the doctor looked her in the eye and told her his condition was worsening, she tried so hard to push it away. She turned to drugs, numbed the idea that he was gone forever. But now, she was having to face it. Sunwoo was gone.

Sunwoo was dead.

She was crying again thinking about it, her eyes looking to her feet in embarrassment. Her older brother looked at her, feeling guilty about his actions. With a sigh, he reached out to take the car keys from the girl's hand, but before she could cry in anger, he'd sent her a small reassuring smile, "alright, come on, I'm going with you."

Solar sent him a small, thankful grin. Jaehyun only patted her head like a pet, before spinning on his heel and heading out into the hallway. Solar turned to glance at Jinhwan and Jungkook, motioning for them to follow. The two hurriedly rushed after her as they left the apartment.

"Wait, what the fuck - I'm coming too, then!" Chaerin had yelled, throwing her purse over her shoulder and bolting to join the group walking out the door, "Might as well make it a gathering."

And before you knew it, Hyunjoon and Chanhee were coming, too. 

It left a feeling of warmth inside of her, knowing that people who never got along their entire lives, people that were polar opposites, were brought together - even if it was by a horrible event like this. She was happy to see her siblings, old best friends, and Sunwoo's gang members all getting along. More so, she was happy to see Jungkook and Jinhwan get along - that was a miracle within itself.

"I'm sorry, sister," Chaerin had been mumbling to her as they walked outside the apartment complex, her arm over her shoulder, "you were so upbeat because of the drugs, I didn't want to set you off. When I found out, I cried like a fucking idiot," she managed to chuckle, "that's so fucking embarrassing to say out loud - I cried over Sunwoo."

Solar was still sniffling, her eyes were still puffy and red. But she found a way to smile at her sister's words, "God, Sunwoo would love to hear that one."

She could still see that cocky smile in her head. She could still see the way his eyes would sparkle when he looked at her, like she was something he needed to live, something he'd never let go of. Kim Sunwoo never did let go of her, even after all those years, he continued to live - to go on - with hope that they would reunite again one day.

And they did. Even though it wasn't exactly how he wanted, it wasn't how they planned for it to go, they found each other again. And he fought for her until his very end. 

She was afraid she would never be able to repay him, not for everything he'd done for her. And Solar would have to live with that forever. She'd never get to hear his sarcastic remarks, his dull laugh, or see the way he'd let his stubborn, tough demeanor go when he was around her. 

All the memories felt like poison as they rolled around her brain. The truth was, she had more memories with him than anyone else. She knew him since she was a kid, since their parents met all those years ago, and they became instant friends. She was always in awe by him, by how fast he matured, how smart he was. Sunwoo was like an idol to her, someone she'd always looked up to. 

Now that she thought about it, about the life they had lived together, about all the years they relied on each other, she wished she'd done a lot of things different. That night everything happened, that night her parents kicked her out, she should've gone to him. Things would've turned out different. 

But she could only imagine the differences she could've made.

The ride to the hospital was silent. Jaehyun drove, with everyone else crammed into the seats and even in the very back that didn't have any seats. Solar sat there, leaning her head against the cold window, staring at the street lights they passed. She was trying to prepare herself for what she would see. She was trying to make herself believe she could handle seeing the first boy she ever loved on his death bed. 

Nothing could ever prepare her for that sight. The memories continued to haunt her until she was crying again, silently, the tears streaming down her face and onto her lap. Jinhwan, who was sitting beside her, heard her slight sniffles, and reached out to grab her hand, rubbing the back of it with his thumb to try to ease her nerves. But nothing could really seem to do so that night. 

Their shoes click against the pavement of the parking lot. No one said anymore than Jaehyun asking her if she was sure she wanted to see him, to which she only nodded. 

So, they all walked, until they were getting off the elevator and walking down the designated hallway to the room Sunwoo had been cramped in for months now. Jaehyun led the way, with Hyunjoon right behind him, and everyone else was with Solar, worried about how she would react to everything. 

The oldest brother reached the door first, his hand holding the handle as he turned to look to them. "I - Are you really sure, Solar?" he asked again, the worry evident in his tone, "I-I don't know if it's really safe for you to see him like this..."

"Stop questioning her, it's her right!" Hyunjoon had snapped before she could even say anything, "She needs to see him."

But Jaehyun ignored the sassy boy, only looking to the brunette that was standing behind him. 

"Yeah, it's alright," she assures him, but her voice was weak, barely above even a whisper.

They knew she wasn't really ready to see, but Hyunjoon was right, she deserved to see him before it was too late. His condition was only worsening, according to the doctors, after all. He was on life support, he would be dead any day now. She needed to see him before it was too late.

So, he'd opened the door. It'd creaked against the tiled floor as he'd entered first, with Solar close behind now. All she could think of was how bad she wanted to see him one last time, to just touch his face again. She just wanted to at least talk to him, even if he couldn't talk back. 

She'd entered the room fully, closing her eyes until she felt she was ready to open them.

But when she did, her jaw dropped. 

What exactly was she expecting to see? 

Sunwoo - as pale as a ghost, as dead as a human could ever look, practically a pile of bones laying there in the bed, with endless amounts of machines and tubes hooked up to him. Sunwoo, the heartless gang boss, that somehow loved her with everything inside of him. She expected to see something along those lines, to at least see him sleeping peacefully.

Not to not see him at all.

"WHERE IS HE?!" she turned and screamed at the top of her lungs. Her first thought was that he was dead, so dead that he'd already been shipped away, maybe back to America to be buried or cremated. She couldn't imagine his ashes being given to his father, to the horrible man that made him the way he was, that ruined his life. She couldn't imagine Sunwoo being gone, being buried in the ground to never see again. She didn't want to. She didn't prepare for that. She was so unprepared, in fact, that she couldn't stop herself from screaming the words over and over. "WHERE IS HE?! TELL ME, WHAT ELSE ARE YOU HIDING FROM ME?!" 

"Solar, Solar!" Chanhee had been lecturing her as everyone else had finally entered.

"What the hell are you talking about?" Chaerin had cried.

"What the fuck..." Jaehyun was muttering, and everyone else had followed his eyes to look to the hospital bed that was previously filled. 

There was nothing there.

"That doesn't make any fucking sense!" Chaerin exclaimed, "I was - I was here, they told me themselves he was on fucking life support!"

"Did anyone tell them to pull the plug?" Chanhee is asking, glancing to Hyunjoon or one of the siblings, "They said we needed to come to a decision, right? Maybe someone said something when they came to visit."

"No," Hyunjoon quickly asserted, "none of us would've ever told them to do such a thing."

Solar was crying, she was crying harder than she had in a while. Poor Jinhwan and Jungkook were too focused on trying to calm her down to pay attention to the conversation. 

"His dad!" Solar was screaming, "I bet his dad found out - I bet they contacted him! He probably had him killed!" she burst into more sobs, loud sobs that were likely echoing down the entire hall. She just couldn't fathom it, but she knew it made the most sense. It wasn't like Sunwoo magically got better when he was on life support. It had to be his father. It had to be.

Unless someone else had gotten to him.

"It could be several things..." Jaehyun sighed, burying his face in his hands, obviously rather stressed, "this is insane... I'm going to try to find a doctor to talk to..."

Him and Chaerin disappeared outside to do just that, leaving everyone else to sit in the now unoccupied hospital room. 

Hyunjoon had been investigating, looking around the bed to see if any of his belongings were left. But it appeared the entire room was wiped clean, as if no one had ever been there in the first place. 

"Solar, honey," he'd cooed as he made his way back to the sobbing girl. The two boys comforting her had moved aside so he could pull her into a hug, "calm down, alright? This doesn't mean what you think it means, sweetie, it could mean something better."

"It wouldn't make any sense..." is all she gets out, burying her face into the boy's hoodie, "do you guys have a way to contact his piece of shit father?" she sniffled, "Ask him if he knows anything..."

He thought for a minute, before shrugging, "not exactly, you know that sick asshole only really talks through Sunwoo."

"Surely, they'll hear something from the doctor," Chanhee advised with a sigh, pacing around the hospital room, "either way, this makes no sense."

"He could be alive though, right?" Jinhwan speaks up, trying to lighten the mood in anyway he could.

"I don't see how it would be possible," Hee shook his head, "the idiot was dying, he was on life support..."

Before anyone could say more, the door has burst open, and in came Jaehyun and Chaerin. They looked as if they'd both just ran miles, with red cheeks and heavy breathing. The thing was, their expressions were riddled with anger, and it left Solar with a sour taste in her mouth. 

"They wouldn't tell us anything," Chaerin is the first to spout in clear annoyance, "can you fucking believe that? They said it was confidential what happened to him."

"As if we haven't been the main contacts for him the last three months," Jaehyun rolled his eyes, "something real fucking weird is going on."

"I advise we go home," Hyunjoon hums, "tell the others."

"Yeah," Jaehyun sighed, "that's the best idea..."


174:00 | off the rails

Solar was off the rails. 

"I just need to be by myself," she'd insisted to everyone once they got back to the apartment. She didn't really care for what they all had to say or to hear them talk about it anymore. Her entire wellbeing was falling apart. It was bad enough she felt guilty for how distracted she'd gotten because of the drugs, and now Sunwoo was just gone - dead - nowhere to be found? The only thing she could think was he'd died, so they sent him back where he came from. According to Chanhee, the doctors were clear when they said his heart was failing and he'd be switching to life support soon. No one just casually recovered from that and mysteriously disappeared. 

She had to figure out how she would face Sunwoo's death. And doing so meant she needed to have a long talk with herself, cry it out, do more drugs until the memories went away. 

"Sol, I don't think that's very safe..." Jacob had been the first to voice his concern. 

"I have a weapon on me," she was deadpanning, already walking out the door, "be back in a bit."

And they'd let her leave. Everyone seemed to understand she really did need to be alone after that. They knew how hard it was all hitting her, and it was hard - even with the cocaine in her system, she still felt the pain. She had all this adrenaline, telling her she needed to kill that man, to rip his insides open and laugh as he died. She needed to do horrible things to these people that worked for him, people that wanted to ruin her, people that were the reason Sunwoo was dead. 

He wasn't a good person. He killed people, even innocent people, but he was the first boy she ever looked at and knew she loved. He protected her ever since she was a little kid, shielded her from the bad, and that was what he died doing.

She knew it was her fault, that was the worst part of it all.

Solar was dead quiet as she sat in the elevator, watching the big number go down to the first floor. There wasn't a bone in her body that was afraid anymore. She didn't care. She felt like she had nothing more to lose. All she wanted was for those six days to pass so it would all be over, she swore she would end it. 

Her own brain was like poison. Her hands shuffle around her jacket pockets, making sure she had what she needed. With that, she made her way through the apartment lobby and outside into the landscaping area. The YG apartment building was fenced off, and it had a nice park area right outside. 

Sure, there was still a chance someone would see her. There was still a chance there was some freak that was watching her every move. But she didn't care anymore. She didn't care to be cautious and careful. If someone wanted something from her, they could come and get it.

So, she found a nice tree in the middle of the park area, letting her back slide against it until her bottom hit the ground. There wasn't too much light in the area, and it was still dark since it was the middle of the night after they'd gotten back from the hospital, so she felt like she had enough privacy. 

Taking the small baggy out of her pocket, she took out a plastic straw she'd brought. This wasn't the typical method she used, but surely, it would be good enough. No doubt, it was going to hit her like a truck - which was exactly what she was hoping for. 

Carefully, she dumped some of it into the palm of her hand. About to get right into action, her green eyes ended up lingering on the sight for a minute. 

Her brain was scattered. All she could see in her head was Sunwoo. He was all she could think about, what his dead body must look like. She wondered what his last thoughts were, probably something about how he just wanted to make sure she was safe, make sure she lived. 

And she had, but at what cost when he hadn't?

She was crying before she could stop herself, as a wet tear slides down her cheek and into the palm of her hand. Not wanting to show anymore weakness, she squeezed her eyes shut, leaning her head back onto the tree. 

She swore she could hear his voice in her head, like he was sitting right beside her, like he was standing in front of her, that cocky smile on his face. 

"I'm proud of you, sweetheart... I don't want you to make a mistake and ruin all you've achieved... you know what drugs do to people... are you sure you would be able to handle them anymore?"

Sunwoo, Sunwoo, the boy that never failed to assure her that he loved her. His words repeated in her head over and over until she felt like she was being taunted by his ghost. She found herself choking on her own tears when she opened her eyes, how could he really be gone? 

He was a crime boss, a gang leader, he'd killed more people than she could imagine. He dealt drugs, but still, he never wanted her to do them. He tried to keep her away from his lifestyle for the longest time, until he realized it was impossible, she would always be apart of it because of her parents business. Until she tried drugs for the first time, and then the second, before she was hooked. Even then, he tried to get them away from her. But she always found a way. 

It was weird, weird to think he wasn't there to take them away from her anymore. She always took his word over everything because of who he was, he just seemed to know the most. 

Sniffling, it felt like there was tsunami in her nose. Solar kicked a rock by her feet, before shrugging, she couldn't help herself. They were calling to her, it was the only thing that would make it hurt less. 

So, with a deep breathe, she held up the straw to her nostril over her hand, and snorted the amount of powder she'd poured into her palm. She had to sniffle a lot more than usual, trying to get it all in her system. After a bit of coughing from some getting stuck in her throat, she was good. 

And then she sat there in the dark, staring up at the sky and wondering if he was looking down at her from somewhere in the stars. 

Until, a certain idea struck her brain, until she thought of something that would help her speak her thoughts, let out her pain. 

But Solar hadn't brought anything with her. She dug in her pockets, finding a lucky pen and a napkin. Figuring it would be good enough, she brought her knee up to set the napkin on top of it, leaning forward to scribble the words that came to her mind, and they flew like silk. 

Because Solar had a story to tell, she had a message to relay. Sunwoo's story. 

She thought of it all, what she'd been through, the things she could never say. And somehow, she would fit them into the right words. 


Keep your helmet, keep your life, son

Just a flesh wound, here's your rifle

"Sir, I think he's bleeding out..."

And some things, you just can't speak about


Something med school did not cover,

Someone's daughter, someone's mother,

Holds your hand through plastic now

"Doc, I think she's crashing out..."

And some things, you just can't speak about

Only twenty minutes to sleep,

But you dream of some epiphany

Just one single glimpse of relief

To make some sense of what you've seen

With you I serve,

With you I fall down,

Watch you breath in, watch you breathing out



Solar might've had the hearing of a bat. A sudden rustle from somewhere behind her caught her immediate attention, and like a spy, she whipped the weapon in her pocket out, aiming it straight at whoever it may be. She swore no one would be fucking with her anytime soon. 

"Um... Solar?!" the boy had gawked, putting his hands up in surprise at the fact his friend was holding out a live weapon at him.

Her green eyes widened, and she cleared her throat, shoving it back into the pocket. "Jihoon... I apologize for that," she muttered, sniffling a bit since her nose was still stuffy and turning back around to get comfortable again, "I forgot you lived in the same building - I'm a little uh... on edge."

"I didn't think you would be out here alone," he admits, clearly a little confused as he sat on the grass next to her, "this may be creepy - but one of my friends said they saw you through the window, so I thought I'd come check on you."

She only shrugged, not having too much energy to even stifle a laugh. "Oh, that's nice of you."

Jihoon picked up her lack of enthusiasm, it was definitely out of place. "Do you want to talk about it?" he decided to pend, "I know I'm still technically an outsider, but it might be good to get some stuff of your chest."

She thought about it for a while, before deciding she might as well. She trusted Jihoon. "Well, remember the guy at the hospital?" she started, and Jihoon had nodded, "Right, I doubt Jungkook told you much - I uh - I grew up with him, knew him since I was a kid, dated him for seven years until I left America at 18 and cut contact..." she cleared her throat, kicking her feet around in the grass as a distraction while she went on, "I'm sure you noticed, he was the leader of something a little illegal... but he was still the sweetest guy I ever knew... I kept telling myself he would wake up, that he'd sit up in that hospital bed and laugh at me."

The boy next to her didn't say anything, he just nodded along to show he was listening. 

"I - um, I got a little distracted, didn't visit him for a while... according to my friend, Chanhee, the doctors told him that he was in critical condition - heart failure, they said," she exhaled, "so - so I went to see him, wanted to prepare myself and say my goodbyes, I guess."

"That must've been hard."

"Well, it would've been," she deadpanned, "but he was gone, entire room empty like he was never there."

"Oh," Jihoon was in clear shock, "how is that possible?"

Sniffling loudly, she shook her head, "I have no fucking idea, Jihoon."

"It doesn't necessarily mean the worst, right?"

"Sure, Sunwoo was never one to just give up," she mumbled, "but it wouldn't make any sense, that means he would've basically waken up from the dead."

Jihoon pursed his lips, in deep thought. He could tell Solar was out of it, that she was crying, and he wanted to cheer her up in anyway he could.

"Well, miracles do happen when you least expect them."


175:00 | honoring someone beautiful

"Are you sure you're okay?" Jaehyun had been more so demanding than anything when Solar had come back to the apartment that night.

She didn't even look at him, nor anyone else. Solar just stared at her feet as she pushed passed everyone, head hanging low it might as well had touched the ground. And she blankly, incoherently muttered in response, "yeah... yeah, I'm fine."

Then, she'd disappeared into her bedroom and closed the door behind her. Jungkook and Jinhwan both tried to go after her, but Chanhee stopped them, saying they should give her a break - and that if anyone was going to talk to her, it would be him. They knew all they could do was listen to the best friend, because all hell would break loose if they didn't.

"Well, I have a schedule tomorrow," Jinhwan had explained, "but tell Soo to call me, I'll stop by as soon as I can."

He'd bid everyone goodbye before leaving. The gang members, siblings, and Jungkook were the only ones still there. 

No one really knew what to do about Solar. She was mourning. They all knew the evitable, they all knew the answer to what happened to Sunwoo - or more so, what likely happened. There were several possibilities, but the ones that made the most sense were ones where he was dead. 

There was just no other explanation.

Solar knew that better than anyone else. So, she kept snorting the drugs, kept trying to forget about it the best she could. But the more she tried, the guiltier she felt. It was like she was trying to erase him from her memory, and that wasn't really what she wanted. She just wanted the pain to stop.

The morning finally rolled around, with her not getting more than a few hours of sleep. Still, she'd made her way into the living room after a morning pick up. No longer did she care to look normal, since everyone would just assume her broken attitude was from what happened. She figured they would be off her back about the drugs.

"Hey, Sol, sit down," Chanhee had urged her as soon as he saw her entering, walking over to guide her with his hand on her back, as if she was unable to walk herself. 

The girl didn't say anything, though. She just let him guide her until she fell onto one of the sofas. Her best friend had sat beside her, wrapping an arm around her shoulders and hugging her tightly. 

Her expression was blank. Not everyone was awake yet, it was just her siblings and some of the gang members. Jungkook was likely asleep in the guest bedroom. There was a heavy silence that fell over everyone as they all began getting situated.

Until Sangyeon had finally cleared his throat, speaking into the silence, "It's likely we all understand the inevitable..." his voice trailed off into the dull air. No one said anything, no one moved a muscle. The gang members all looked as if they'd been ripped into pieces, like this was the first time they'd ever shown emotion in their lives. Most of them had grimaces on, hanging their heads low so no one would see them cry. They were messes, products of destruction, because that day it had finally sunk in that they'd lost not only their leader, but the key to their family. Sunwoo had saved them, they idolized him, they looked up to him - and now he was gone. 

"He's not dead..." Eric had whispered, just loud enough for everyone to hear. But it sounded like he was telling himself more than anyone else, like he was trying to convince himself, as if he was having an argument with his own mind, "he can't be dead..."

It wasn't surprising, most of them were in a trance, trying to understand. Solar shook her head, the guilt covering her like a blanket as she tried to ignore the way they all looked. She'd never seen them like this in all the years she'd known them, and she never planned to. 

And the fact it was all her fault was the worst part. 

"This is difficult for all of us," Sangyeon slowly went on, "but we have to keep our heads up and ours eyes on the mission..." he paused, eyes raking over everyone's condition. After a second of hesitation, he posed, "but... I thought it would only be right to hold a memorial, perhaps our own funeral, for the man that saved us all when we needed him - to our leader."

Everyone slowly lifted their heads up to look to him. Their eyes were red, some puffy, some even had tears streaming down their cheeks. But the idea of honoring Sunwoo made them smile. He deserved that, more than anything. 

The looks on their faces seemed to show that they agreed. 

So, the rest of the day, they all worked on setting up a memorial in Solar's apartment. She had cleaned off a small table that sat by the window, overlooking the city of Seoul, to place everything that reminded them of him. There were some things she didn't have access to since they were back at her home in New York, but she hoped she would be able to dig up enough to make it feel right. 

The next few hours were spent with her kneeled on the carpet in her bedroom, digging through boxes of pictures. When she came back from New York, she had brought some pictures and momentums that she wanted to have in her new home. Though, she had yet to quite go through all of them.

She'd finally found pictures from when she was in high school. She'd coughed a bit from the dust that was flying around her since they were quite old now, but couldn't care less as she smiled down at them. Everywhere she went, Sunwoo was by her side. They were always glued to the hip, since they were ten years old. She was always the star in his eyes.

After picking out some pictures, she'd made her way over to her dresser. Her hand is shaky as it reaches for the shining rings atop the furniture, and she already felt the urge to cry when she picked them up. She had both of them now, her and Sunwoo's promise rings. The most heartbreaking part was that Sunwoo's had scratches of blood on it. 

They shone in the palm of her hand, and she squeezed her eyes shut, closing her palm and holding it to her chest. She stood there for a while, as if she was hugging the rings, because they were one of the most important things she had from him. 

You and me forever. 

She remembered, she remembered the fear that riddled her spine when she walked into that basement and saw him there. And she remembered the way it felt when she killed that man, that man she didn't know. She did it all for him.

Opening her eyes, the vivid memory playing slowly faded away. Letting out a deep breathe, she turned to make her way to the closet, wanting to grab one of his hoodies or something of the sort. After that, the last thing she grabbed was his gun, the one he had used and custom made for himself since he was a teenager. Her hand was shaky when she looked down at it, she knew she would keep it with her for a long time. 

And then, she'd made her way back into the living room. Everyone else was either gathered around the memorial table or sitting on the sofas. They greeted her as she entered, moving out of the way so she could set her things down, too. 

"Aw, Sol..." Changmin had been gushing as he looked down at the pictures she set out, "you guys were so little."

She chuckled a little bit, looking down at them again. "Yeah, we were eleven in that one."

She still couldn't believe she'd known him that long. 

There wasn't a crazy assortment of items other than the ones she'd put. There were his cigarettes, some more of his famously used weapons, but the one that caught her eye the most was the leather jacket they'd laid out. All of the members had a jacket that represented their gang, with the same symbol. But Sunwoo's was designed differently because he was the leader. There were several embroidered symbols and different letters all over it to represent him and who he was. A tear rolled down her cheek as she looked at it, you rarely caught him without that jacket on. And over the years, he'd slowly added more momentums to it. 

As she thought of that, her eyes caught one she hadn't seen before. It looked like a dark circle, with a golden glow around it, like it was carefully embroidered so each string sat just right in the design. She leaned down to investigate it further, knitting her brows in concentration. That was when she noticed two letters that you could only see in the middle of the circle, only if you looked close enough. 

S.L

That was when it hit her - her initials - Solar Lee. It was a solar eclipse.

Solar coughed, she coughed so hard that it started an entire coughing fit. Chanhee was the first to make his way over to her, gently hitting her back to help. She was choking, choking on her own emotions as she turned away from the scene, using the back of her hands to wipe her teary eyes. 

This was horrible. 

"Mi," Jungkook coaxed as he made his way over to the crying girl, "Mi, I'm sorry." 

She accepted his hug, burying her face in his t-shirt. She was glad they were doing this, having this for Sunwoo, it was just so fucking hard for her. 

"Are we all ready to start?" Sangyeon had voiced loudly, earning everyone's attention. 

No one really said much, but everyone looked to agree. 

The friends sat on the sofas along with Jungkook. All of the gang members chose to stand in order to show respect and honor their leader, and Solar decided to stand with them. They looked as if they were pledging allegiance to the flag or something, with the way they all straightened their posture and looked straight ahead. 

"Sunwoo saved me from my abusive family when I was 14 years old," Sangyeon had cleared his throat, standing beside the memorial table and looking down at it with solace, "he took me in, offered me a home, a new life. He taught me how to defend myself, gave me the means to teach myself how to hack into mainframes, cameras, whatever I so desired. He provided me with everything I never thought I would have - a family." 

The rest of the members made noises of agreement. 

"He saved us all, and for that, we will forever be grateful," he went on firmly, "Sunwoo will remain the leader of everything we do. We will never forget everything he's sacrificed."

He went on for a while. Solar found herself crying again, sniffling the pain away as she listened to the different members talk about their experiences with Sunwoo. He had saved each of them, whether they were homeless, got disowned, kicked out, abused, kidnapped - you name it. They each had such diverse situations, and Sunwoo had rescued them from them all, gave them a new home, gave them hope when they had one. They were all broken people with nothing that became one, that started a family. 

And that was special. 

"He was a hard ass," Hyunjoon had been saying, shaking his head as he reminsced, "but he was the smartest, best hard ass I've ever fucking known." 

They all made noises of agreement again. 

"Solar," Sangyeon looks to her softly, "would you like to say anything?"

Everyone's eyes felt like lasers when they beamed into her. She didn't look at anyone because she knew her emotions wouldn't be able to handle it. Hesitantly, she sent the boy a nod, before heading to stand beside him. 

"Um..." she stuttered out, green eyes making the insane decision to rake over the friends and members. They were all looking at her, some of them crying, some of them smiling - but no matter what, there were looks of admiration in their eyes. She felt accepted with them, she felt loved. She knew they didn't blame her like she blamed herself, and she was thankful for that. 

"Uh..." she muttered again, trying to find the words she wanted to say, but it seemed they would never come. So, she started talking without thinking about what she was saying, "I met Sunwoo when I was ten years old. He was always... well above his age, smart - I remember thinking he was the coolest boy I'd ever met..." she giggled to herself as the brief memory flashed in her head. 

"I fell in love with him when I was a kid, he was the only person I ever saw myself with. My parents hated him because of his father, because of what he would make him do," she recalled, her voice faltering as she said each word, but she kept on, "but I could never stay away from him. He never failed to remind me he loved me, to tell me he lived to make sure I was safe..."

Tears trickled down her face, but she had so much more to say. "I loved Sunwoo, I loved him with every bone in my body, and if I could go back and do something I would do it three thousand times and over... he was cocky, arrogant, but he showed me what love was. He was the most passionate and caring person I ever met, and he didn't deserve everything his father turned him into..." she let out a small cry without meaning to, the tears seeming to overcome her. Sangyeon, who was beside her, pulled her into a small hug of comfort.

"I will spend the rest of my life honoring him, he stood for the right things, he just wasn't shown the right way to show them always," she sniffled, "but he'll always remain the strongest and most compassionate person I've ever known."

Everyone might as well had been crying now. Solar was emotional, she had so many words she could say about Sunwoo. She knew him better than anyone. She'd seen every side of him. And she knew who he really was inside. She would hold that close for the rest of her life. 

When everyone had said what they wanted to say, Sangyeon had brought our an urn. There was a sort of ritual or tradition that they would do if anyone close to them passed away, especially inside their little family. The thing was, they had never really had to do it before. 

Since they didn't actually have the ashes, it was a bit harder. But Solar had deemed they could use flower petals and light them on fire. Everyone agreed it would work well enough.

He'd set it down in the middle of the coffee table so everyone could gather around. The gang members had all pulled out their lighters, preparing to start the little fire inside of the urn. Solar stood by Jungkook - and Jinhwan, who had arrived in the middle of her speech from his schedule - grabbing onto both of their hands and squeezing them with all of her might. 

And she closed her eyes, closed her eyes so she could imagine actually getting to say the proper goodbye that the two of them had deserved. 

The sound of the members igniting their lighters tickled her ears, and she waited to hear the flames hit the petals. 

But instead, they were all met with a different sound. The sound of an opening door that someone had forgot to lock. 

"Hm, holding a memorial already?"


176:00 | who she is

Solar thought it was the cocaine. 

Sometimes, you feel like you're walking on the clouds - it tends to have that kind of effect. You feel like you could do anything, even if you wanted to overthrow the government and declare war. Everything feels so possible. You're walking in an endless euphoria, and every step you take is a bliss of blossoming glee. 

She knew this feeling well thanks to her reoccurring drug usage. She had also had her fair experiences with PTSD and hallucinations. 

But this was none of those things. And none of those things could prepare her to hear Kim Sunwoo's voice again.

The room grew dead silent. It was likely everyone thought they were hallucinating. They had all come to the same conclusion, the same understanding. Finally, they had let the inevitable sink in. 

A few of the gang members whipped out their weapons, thinking it had to be some kind of game. That maybe this was an enemy, someone trying to trick them in one of the evilest ways possible. 

Solar dared herself to open her eyes. 

Heavy footsteps click against the wooden floor, and the sound of someone dropping something onto it is heard. Still, she didn't move a muscle. She was frozen in time, staring down at the urn that was for her dead first love. 

He was dead.

She knew he was dead.

"What are you all doing?" a dark chuckle is heard as the bag had made a clunk onto the ground, "While I appreciate the gesture, I was out doing more important things."

Kim fucking Sunwoo.

It didn't sink in for a while. Her head slowly lifted, and she slowly turned to bask at what she was hearing. She expected to see a ghost, to see anything that wasn't the taunting brown irises of Kim Sunwoo peering back at her. 

And his chest heaving up and down, he was breathing.

Alive.

He was alive. He was standing right there in the living room of her apartment, a few blood splats on his skin, a black t-shirt clinging to his body. His hair was a mess atop his head, his piercings glistening under the ceiling lights, same earrings dangling from his ears. And the same devious smirk riddled across his lips. 

She couldn't believe it.

Sunwoo chuckled as he stared back at her. "Well, sweetheart, are you gonna gawk at me or come say hello?"

Stupid, annoying, cocky Sunwoo.

But she couldn't contain her excitement.

"SUNWOO!" she screamed, she screamed like a child that was seeing their parents after months of them being away. She screamed, not in pain or agony or anger, but in pure, ecstatic joy. Bliss erupted from her body like confetti, and her feet practically slammed against the floor as she ran into his open arms, "Sunwoo... Sunwoo... you're here, are you really here?" She was blabbering on and on, not being able to believe that she was really touching him, that he was really standing there looking down at her with that same look on his face. 

The boy had continued to snicker to himself as he wrapped his toned arms around her. She squeezed him harder than she'd ever squeezed anyone before. Her fists balled the back of his t-shirt together, almost ripping it apart in that very moment. 

"Sunwoo... how are you here?" she's gasping on, trying to piece the pieces together, "they said you were dying... I thought I lost you..."

"Oh, Solar," he shook his head, "do you really think I would ever leave you?" He would almost sound offended if it wasn't for the fact his voice lacked emotion. With an arm still wrapped around the girl's shoulder, who was still clinging onto him like her life depended on it, he finally looked to everyone else.

And he smiled. 

"Well, look at you emotional idiots," he hummed humorously, "really had the guts to think I left you for good, too?" 

The gang members all made noises of disbelief, before they all practically lunged forward. 

That was the first time she ever saw a bunch of violent gang members have an emotional group hug - that she was caught in the middle of. 

"You're a fucking psycho, man," Eric was gawking, "I thought we lost you for fucking good."

"You're a strong one, Sunwoo," Hyunjoon had sighed next, "fucking insane, but nonetheless, a strong one."

"I'm glad you're alive," Sangyeon was the last to tell him, patting his shoulder, "I'd never be able to lead like you, you're the only real leader here." 

Sunwoo sent him a nod of agreement but also acknowledgement and thanks. "I appreciate that, Sangyeon." 

"So how'd you do it, hm?" Chanhee had crossed his arms over his chest, standing before the crime boss once all the members had stepped away and made some space. He was the only one who didn't look like they just witnessed a literal miracle - because they had. He had made it clear he hated Sunwoo and didn't really feel much about his passing, he had only ever been worried for his best friend. More than anything, he wanted to know how the hell this came about, because he was the one who spoke to the doctor that told him Sunwoo was literally on life support. 

The gang leader chuckled at the flower boy. "What? Not happy to see me?" 

Hee rolled his eyes, scoffing as he turned away, "I am thrilled that you're here to save Solar's wellbeing - to some degree. But I need to know, how is that possible?" he presses on, "I spoke to the doctor, they told me you had heart failure."

Sunwoo shrugged, acting as if he hadn't just been on his death bed. "Guess I had other plans," he hummed like it was no big deal, "can't tell you too much - just woke up one day, got myself discharged with a bunch of medicine - and got to work."

Chanhee was shaking his head in disbelief, "that's fucking absurd."

The boy watched humorously as he walked to sit back down. "I'd never let you have the satisfaction of me being dead, pretty boy."

Solar couldn't help but laugh hearing that name for Chanhee roll of his tongue. She couldn't lie, she missed their bickering, as crazy as it sounded. 

Chanhee clearly hadn't, as he heaved out a sigh, muttering under his breathe about how much he couldn't stand the boy. 

Before anyone could say more, the gang leader had pursed his lips, his brown eyes suddenly catching sight of something across the room. Solar could feel the embarrassment rising in her throat, already knowing what was coming. Sunwoo's combat boots riddled with dirt and grime skid against the wood flooring, and everyone looks with humored eyes as he makes his way across the room to the memorial table by the window. 

The first thing he did was pick up his jacket, clearing his throat as he throws it over his shoulders. A look of satisfaction was on his face as he did so, looking over the sleeves as if he forgot what was on them. No one really noticed much out of place. 

"Hm, look at this," he's humming, "a little memorial all for me, huh?" He'd picked up one of the pictures Solar had set out, one from when they were freshmen. Sunwoo didn't really look that different, but both of them were still babies. A close lipped, devious grin is on his face as he turns to the girl that was still across the room, "Got emotional, didn't you?" He taunted, and she groaned as soon as the words left his mouth.

"You're ridiculous," she's snapping in disbelief as she walked over to him, snatching the picture out of his hands and looking down at it herself. Her smile was so bright in the picture, her green eyes shimmering with happiness just because she had been with him. It was before she found out everything about his life, before she knew any of the dark things. She was just a happy high school girl in love. "I was sad," she mumbled under her breathe so only he could hear, "I thought I really lost you this time."

She was still staring down at the picture, but Sunwoo was staring down at her. 

"I don't break my promises, Solar."

She could feel her emotions getting the best of her again when she looked up at him. That sparkle glistened in his eyes when they met hers, and she felt carefree for a mere moment. She almost felt like she was a freshmen in high school again, looking up at the boy that saved her from all wrong. The boy that never broke a promise. 

When she didn't say anything, Sunwoo chuckled to himself, knowing she was speechless. He reached out to the table again, before holding up a shiny ring, sliding it onto his finger like it never left. "That's why I'm ending this, Sol." 

She knew he meant it that time. She had no idea what he had been doing, or how long he had exactly been awake and discharged. It was still insane to her that he was alive, there was no telling what kind of pain he was in and what kind of crazy medication they had him on. But he would never show any sign of pain. He was a rock, a hard rock that was always strong for everyone else. 

Sunwoo stepped forward to hug her, kissing her forehead before he turned to his gang members. She listened to the sound of his footsteps walking away, as if she was trying to make sure they were real. She realized then, she might've taken Sunwoo for granted - probably her entire life. Losing him for real had showed her a lot of things, a lot of things she had never thought about. Sometimes, when you know someone for so long, you don't think about what they've really done for you, you don't think about how special they are, you don't think about how much you really love them.

You don't think about how you really feel, not until you feel them slipping from your grasp. 





+




"Is it really okay for you to be doing that?" Jinhwan asked nervously, not wanting to overstep a boundary, but also knowing that if it was something Solar shouldn't be doing, Chanhee would kill him. 

The brunette let out a tired sigh as she leaned against the brick wall of the apartment building, kicking the back of her heel on it to distract herself momentarily. Her green eyes watch the smoke as she blows it into the air, coughing a bit because she still wasn't too used to it still. Only then, did she hum, "yeah, it's weed, Nani. Nothing crazy."

"Sorry," he croaks, knowing he sounded like a bit of a loser for even asking that, "I forget how different it is in the States."

"Mm," she mutters, taking another hit and inhaling deeply. Blowing the smoke into the air again, she looked almost satisfied by the sight, before turning to him, "I know nobody does that here, I was kind of fascinated by it at first. Felt like everywhere I went in New York, someone was doing drugs or killing someone, you know? It was just normal to me," she admitted, looking away and back up to the sky. "Then I came here with nothing, thought to myself - I'll never fit in here - there's no weed, no parties... but losing all of those things that were normalized to me made me find myself."

He was silent for a while. "That's great, Soo."

"Is it?" she asked no one in particular, shaking her head vigorously, "I don't feel like it's that great, I don't feel like I really know who it is that I found in myself. I just feel like I molded myself into what I thought people wanted me to be, just so I could be successful... but is that really me?"

"Do you feel like it is?"

"No," she deadpanned, reaching the end of her blunt before she chunked it to the ground. "I still feel like I'm just making myself fit in sometimes," she sighed, "as horrible as it is, I didn't have to do that there. That's why I'm so comfortable with those people, those people that do awful unimaginable things - they don't judge me. They don't care if I've killed someone, they don't care if I do drugs, they don't care if I'm not wearing makeup today, they don't fucking care."

"I know it's hard here, Soo," he nods empathetically, "now that you've had such a taste of home, it only makes sense you'd feel that way. It's your home for a reason." He paused for a minute, debating if he wanted to ask, before clearing his throat, "but... what exactly brought these thoughts upon you?"

"I don't know," she shrugged, her back scraping against the wall until her bottom hit the ground, "Sunwoo being alive, seeing him again really made me think, you know? I'm just... a little lost."

"There's a lot on your plate," Jinhwan affirms as he sat on the grass next to her, "the next few days are gonna be hard, there's no telling what'll happen."

His statement made her think. "That's exactly it, Jinhwan."

His eyes look to her with wonder. 

"There's no telling what'll happen after this..." she breathes, "there's no telling how it'll change me, what happens that day will change the course of everything." 

Jinhwan was starting to get an idea of what she meant the more he heard her speak. Pursing his lips, he thought for a moment, before he couldn't hold it in anymore. "Soo, are you thinking about moving back to the States?"

She laughed. 

"You never know, Nani," she threw her arms in the air, stretching them out above her head like she was hugging the sky above her, "never know where the world will call me next."

He knew she was high, so she didn't really have a lot of conscious thought on what she was saying. She didn't have any reason or rationality. However, he also knew there was truth in her words, that she was missing the feeling of home and everything it brought her. She was missing feeling like she had to pretend to be someone that wasn't really her. 

Sure, Kim Soomi was who Solar became, she was the icon she molded herself into to fit the perfect image of a popular Korean idol. She was bright, happy, carefree - but elegant and polite. She did no wrongs, she loved everyone, she had no enemies. She was the girl that Solar would've liked to become, and she pretended so hard she thought she almost did. 

But at the end of the day, all she was ever doing was running. She was running from the darkness of her past, from the demons that haunted her, and everyone knows you can only run so long from something so dark. Until it catches you full force, until it engulfs you, chokes you and forces you to remember that that isn't you. She wasn't Kim Soomi, she wasn't perfect, and she wasn't polite and elegant. 

Solar Lee was the opposite, and as much as she ran from it, Solar Lee was who she would always be. 


177:00 | to feel needed

"You can go home now," Sunwoo had been monotoning, crossing his arms over his chest and leaning his side on the wall of the apartment hall, "I appreciate you looking over her - but I can take it from here."

Jungkook pursed his lips as he stood there, right outside Solar's bedroom. He wasn't sure what to say to Sunwoo, he was glad he alive since they needed him and he knew he was the best guy for the job. Jungkook still felt guilty about not knowing about Solar's drug problem - he knew he could've and should've done something to stop it. And now that it was apparently over, he still felt weary, like he already failed as the one who was supposed to be there for her when no one else was. 

He didn't want to leave, he loved being close to her. He just loved her. He found himself wondering if he should've accepted her proposal for them to get back together all that time ago, where they would be at now. But he knew her mind was in other places now, he knew she didn't really need to be with anyone. He also was smart enough to know that if Solar loved him, she loved Sunwoo just as much if not more. And then there was Jinhwan, the idiot he couldn't stand that would never seem to get out of the picture. 

As much as he didn't want to, he was beginning to realize that Solar Lee would never be tied down. She wasn't who he thought she was, she was something so much bigger than just a famous idol from the States. And he admired her for everything she'd been through. 

With that in mind, he knew it would only be right for him to stand down.

"Alright," he'd finally nodded to Sunwoo. He could see a hint of surprise in the boy's irises, as if he wasn't expecting him to casually agree, yet his face remained blank. "I just have to get my bag and I'll say goodbye to her on the way out," he cleared his throat. Jungkook was about to turn to grab his things from her room, when he stopped himself, slowly turning back to Sunwoo. "Hey, man, I feel like I should tell you..." he hesitated, "Soomi's drug problem got a little out of hand while you were gone," he explained gradually, "I don't know the exact details, and I feel awful for not realizing it sooner... her and Chanhee had a big incident about it, and apparently she's off them... but I don't really know for sure," he sighed, scratching the back of his head, "you're the best person to tell, thought you should know."

The mafia leader slowly nodded as he took in the information. He was very unhappy about it, clearly, but was thankful for Jungkook informing him of such. "Thanks," he mutters, "I can handle it, don't worry about her."

Jungkook only nodded before he disappeared into her bedroom to gather his things. He trusted Sunwoo because he knew that was the truth. He was the only one who had ever solved her drug addiction before, he was practically the key. 

And he would solve it. He didn't mind her occasional use for fun, and he would admit he supplied her a little too much when she first came back to New York. He'd got caught up in the excitement of being with her again, so much so he forgot that it wasn't 2012 anymore. They weren't two dumb teenagers, but full grown adults who couldn't do things like that anymore. He knew they were bad for her, and he knew what they did to her - the kind of person they made her. 

He wouldn't allow her to fall ill to drugs ever again. 

So, he'd spun on his heel to head back into the living room. The gang members were rather calmed now, most of them hanging out doing just that - drugs, or smoking weed. They had just spoke about and finalized the plan. Sunwoo had updated them on what he'd been up to since he got out - which was catching everyone that still worked under Solar's stalker at YGE. He'd hacked into their networks and found evidence of their involvement in the outbreak that occurred where several people died, including their plan to kill Solar. With that evidence, he threatened to turn them all in to the police if they didn't cut ties with the ex manager and leave the country immediately. 

It wasn't necessarily how he liked to solve things, but he knew stuff was different in Korea. It was the most convenient way to get them out of Solar's hair, so she could at least be fully secure at her job. That meant the inside connection was gone, and if he had anyone left working for him, the numbers were slim. 

He was pleased to hear that Hyunjoon had kidnapped the rest of his family, and was holding them in a rented warehouse outside the city. A few of the gang members stayed there regularly, making sure no one found them. The kidnapped family was part of their leverage, how they would make sure the guy would show up. If he played any funny moves, they'd kill them in a heartbeat. 

As far as what they would do when they finally met with him, Sunwoo was still thinking on it. He wanted to kill him. Every single bone in his body wanted to kill him, he just wasn't sure how. Something inside of him felt like it wouldn't be over if he just killed him, something was telling him there was somehow more to the story, that there were still a few puzzle pieces missing. 

"Don't fucking supply her anymore, heard?" he rose a brow at Hyunjoon as he'd stood in front of him. The boy was lounging on the couch, blowing smoke rings into the air, when Sunwoo's words entered his ears.

Jungkook was leaving behind them, waving goodbye to everyone as he lunged his bag over his shoulder. Hyunjoon's eyes landed on him, and he realized that the boy must've informed Sunwoo of what was going on with Solar. He was not very thrilled, he had always had trouble with his leader about supplying her, even back when they were in high school. It wasn't his fault that every time she couldn't get it from Sunwoo or some other random dealer - she would run straight to him. What could he do, say no? Of course not.

"Oh, Sunwoo," he heaved out a tired sigh, "you know how she is."

The leader narrowed his eyes at him. "Yes, I do know how she is, that's why I'm telling you I better not find out you're continuing to supply her."

Even if he didn't want to, he didn't have a choice but to listen to Sunwoo. "Okay, okay, captain," he shrugs, "calm down, she hasn't asked me for anything since Chanhee found out."

Sunwoo exhaled deeply, he wasn't sure if that was good news or bad news. If Solar was using regularly - and heavily - she wouldn't just be abruptly stopping like nothing. And he knew Choi Chanhee wouldn't have had the brains to know every place a drug addict hides their stashes.

So, he had a feeling there might still be an issue. 

The apartment door was opening, much to his luck, minutes later. Solar and Jinhwan entered, with the girl looking as if she just came back from candy land, with how bouncy her steps were. He exhaled, not having to think about it to realize she was stoned. 

"Sweetheart," he was deadpanning as he made his way over to her, "come with me."

Before she could even say a word, he took her hand, dragging her away down the hall and into her bedroom. 

"Uh, Sunwoo?!" she was crying, but didn't oblige until they were finally in her bedroom. He closed the door behind them, finally letting her go and crossing his arms over his chest, turning to her with an expectant look on his face. "What - what is it?!" she blurted in confusion, "why are you looking at me like that?"

He knew she would try to play dumb. It was very like her.

"Where is it, princess?" he hums, pacing around her room almost tauntingly as he waited for a response.

"Where is what?"

Sunwoo sighed impatiently, annoyed that she had the audacity to play dumb with him, as if they hadn't been through the exact same situation three hundred times and over. "I was informed of the incident prior to my return," he explains, "I know you're still using, Solar, and this is me cutting you off, so tell me where your stashes are before I find them myself - and you know I will."

Realizing she was caught, she groaned. Solar shamefully walked to her bed, flailing back onto the mattress like a fish and spreading her arms out by her sides. She hated when Sunwoo did this, he made her feel like she was a kid that got caught by an adult doing something bad. When in reality, he was literally a drug dealer himself. It would never make sense to her. 

"Seriously, Sunwoo?" she blustered, "We're still doing this? I'm fine, the heroin might've got a little out of hand - but I'm not doing it anymore."

"Heroin?!" Sunwoo turned to her in bewilderment, snorting as he shook his head, "Jesus fucking Christ, Solar, so what are you using now then?" he stared at her for a minute, and when she didn't answer, he deadpanned, "Cocaine, it's cocaine, isn't it?"

She hated how he always knew.

"Why does it matter?!" she whined, "It's the only thing that helped, it's been a lot. I had to get you being dead and all this shit off my mind somehow."

"That's the problem, Sol, it shouldn't be the only thing that helps anymore," he snapped, "one of those fucking idiots that you keep around for God knows what should be what helps. What the fuck else are they good for if it isn't supporting you through this?!"

Ouch. 

He was right, and she didn't want to say he was right, but he was. 

"They help in their own ways," she mumbled. But she knew that if Jungkook had really been what she needed, she probably wouldn't have turned to them again. He was just sort of caught up in his own world, in wanting them to be together again so bad. And she didn't blame him for that, because for a while, that was all she cared about, too. 

As for Jinhwan, she had forced him out of the picture, but she didn't care to explain that. But she didn't really have much of an excuse anymore. All she could do was mutter, "old habits die hard... you know that better than anyone, Sunwoo."

His expression softened from her words. With a heavy sigh, he padded across the carpet until he fell onto the mattress beside her. Solar had sat up by now, and Sunwoo wraps an arm around her shoulder, forcing her to lay her head on his chest. 

"I know, sweetheart, I'm not mad at you," he calmly explained, "but you know how it's always been, you know what they do to you, and for your health you know I can't let you rely on them like that."

"I know..." she responded dully. She hated how well he knew her, the real her. She hated how she couldn't hide it from him, because Solar had enjoyed being in her own bubble of euphoria, no matter how unhealthy it was. It had been nice. 

But reality was back, and it was harsher than ever.

"Behind the bathroom mirror and on top of the cabinets, hm?" 

She only had to glance up at him for him to know he got it right. Sunwoo chuckled, shaking his head in disbelief, "Solar Lee, you never change."

"I'm starting to realize that, too," she admitted. 

Laying her head on his chest again, she suddenly realized she felt something abnormal. She seemed to have somehow forgotten about the fact that Sunwoo had been on his death bed, subconsciously raising her hand to graze across his t-shirt, when she heard Sunwoo let out a slight wince.

"Princess, careful there," he whispered under his breathe.

It took her a minute to process what she had been touching. 

"Sunwoo..." she gawks, looking up to the blank expression on his face before back down to his chest. Unable to control her temptation, she lifted up the front of the boy's t-shirt, her eyes widening once she saw the giant red scar across the middle of his chest. 

"Sol," her name flew off his lips like butter, yet he said it with warning in his tone, letting her know he didn't want her to bring up the subject. Though, once her green irises kept pouring into his with that same look of concern and shock, he realized she wasn't going to drop it. 

"You got a transplant," she was dumbfounded, processing the how of how Sunwoo had survived, "Jesus Christ, Sunwoo..."

The boy raises a brow, not seeming to understand her reaction. "What, did you think God himself magically healed me?" he snorted, "Yes, Sol, I had heart transplant surgery."

"Jeez, you're lucky, Sunwoo," is all she could seem to say. It was insane to her, what were the chances of them finding someone? He was lucky to be there right now, and she was still having a hard time processing how true that statement was. 

She was glad he was there. 

"I missed you," she blurted after a moment of silence, "I never thought I'd need you again, Sunwoo, but I do and I missed you."

It was something special to see the rare occasions that Kim Sunwoo showed emotion. She swore the older that they got, the less it was there. But then sometimes, when it was just the two of them, Sunwoo seemed like the cheeriest guy on Earth. He would smile, smile from ear to ear, and the corners of his eyes would crinkle, his eyes would glisten. And for once, he looked like a normal person, a normal happy person that had seen nor done any bad. 

She liked to see him that way. 

Sunwoo had sighed, breaking their eye contact as he thought about her words for a moment. He knew she was still high, so she wasn't really thinking before she was speaking. Her mind was in a happier place, distracted from all the horrible things going on. She was almost carefree - that along with the fact he knew she was still on drugs, which he would be stopping now. 

When he looked at her, she was already staring up at him, and he swore he could see that twinkle in her eyes that she used to get when they were teenagers. He promised to himself again that he would end all the trouble in her life. He would get rid of all the bad until he could lay her down in a field of flowers and tell her she was free, she could do whatever her heart pleased, go wherever she wanted to go. And he swore he would protect her, protect her how he wished he could've seven years ago. He'd do it, again and again. He'd do anything for Solar Lee.

"You and me forever, princess."





178:00 | learning from a trained criminal

The closer the day came, the more anxious Solar got. Sunwoo meant it when he said he was getting rid of all of her stashes - he didn't miss any like Chanhee. She didn't have much to help her, except for the occasional blunt she would get from the members. But it was clear their leader wouldn't allow any of them to give her the slightest taste of a hard drug. 

Still in clear withdrawal, she felt jumpier and more irritable than usual. She was thankful she was at least free from any schedules this week, because the last thing she wanted to do was be at music bank. 

She had heard the plan from the members so many times over she could practically recite it word for word. It was the only thing she could think about, work was the last thing on her mind. She was just lucky that the Jungkook scandal didn't have any traction anymore, likely because they were both such popular idols people would expect them to have some kind of relations - especially on top of the fact they had already had the scandal occur before. The Soomi and Jungkook scandal was still old news, and she was glad people reacted that way. 

Sure, she still received her fair amount of hate from some weird fans. But it was nothing like last time when she was attacked by every netizen there was. 

The problem was that she knew the Jungkook scandal was the lightest trick he had up his sleeve. This person, the person she once trusted that had betrayed her, had dug up everything there was to know about her. They knew about her past, they knew about the gang, they knew about her drug usage, they knew it all. At any given moment, they could destroy her - and not just her career, they could send her to jail for life in Korea. All they had to do was send in the evidence to any news station, any police station, and she was done for. 

Though the members assured her that wouldn't happen before the big day since they had leverage, and his family members would be dead if he caused anything that big. But how could she be so sure? He was reckless, it was likely he didn't even care about his own family members, that his mind was poisoned with the craving of revenge, the need to avenge his wife that was dead by their hands. 

Her foot angrily tapped up and down against the carpet, her legs hanging their off the side of her bed. Green eyes full of concern and worry, she had been staring ahead at the blank wall of her bedroom for what felt like hours, when her door had whipped open.

"Here, sweetheart," Sunwoo came in, holding out a couple of pills to her. She had only stared down at his hand that held them out to her, slowly looking from the pills that sat there, back up to him. 

"Huh?" she blurted, confused as to what he was giving her. 

"I know you're having those muscle aches," he states matter-of-factly, "pain killers, Sol, take them. They help your withdrawal, remember?"

Ah, Sunwoo, the boy that knew everything about her withdrawals. Shaking her head in disbelief, she smiled giddily as she accepted the pain killers, tilting her head back and dry swallowing them without another thought. 

A satisfied look on his face, the boy had sat next to her on the mattress, the plush sinking them both due to the added weight. "Still worrying about the big day?" 

She didn't have to say anything for him to know that was it.

"Sol, listen to me," he demands sternly, reaching out to grab her hand. Sunwoo squeezed it tighter than he ever had, looking to her with more emotion than she'd seen in his eyes in a long time, "I'm not letting anything happen to you, this is the end, alright? It's gone on for long enough, there's no way he would be bold enough to pull anything else. He should know it's his end if he wants to save his family."

But she couldn't drop the inevitable question. "What if he doesn't, Sunwoo? What if there's more? What if he could careless about his family?"

The boy looked away from her, pursing his lips in thought. It was something he'd been thinking about, the fact his gut kept telling him this wasn't the end, but he couldn't seem to figure out what the final trick would be. What was his hidden card? There had to be more to it, something he just wasn't figuring out. And it was killing him, because he was never caught on a limb like this, caught in the dark by anyone, he was trained too well for that. And here he was, feeling lost, it was an embarrassment. 

"Solar, I trust you," he hesitantly voices, causing the girl's eyes to widen in fear of what he was about to say to her, "don't tell anyone what I'm going to share with you."

Unsure of what was coming, she shook her head, "Sunwoo..."

But he squeezed her hand even harder to show he was serious. "Look, I think there's something I'm not thinking of, and I can't admit it out loud to anyone else," he exhaled, using his other hand to pull at his dark hair, "when I questioned everyone working under him, they said that they previously had instructions to go with him when he met up with us..." he explained what had been on his mind, "but after I blackmailed them all, told them to leave the country... they told me there was more, someone else behind him that I wouldn't figure out. I thought I would be able to dig it up before the day came, but I can't find anything, I searched all of those workers devices, emails, messages... nothing."

Taking in the new information, she felt a lump forming in her throat as she croaked out, "is it really safe for us then, Sunwoo? Shouldn't you tell the others?"

He'd let her hand go to rub his temples. Poor Sunwoo was blatantly stressed, looking as if he had been thinking about it endlessly - because he had. "I'm going to," he sighs, "there's a chance that yes, he doesn't care about what happens to his family members, or he's going to try something the day of, he has some kind of plan that I haven't prepared for yet. I just have to make sure I think of it before the day comes."

She stared up at him, the boy that was sitting beside her, the boy that was risking so much for her. Solar would be thankful to Sunwoo for a long time, for everything he had always sacrificed in order to save her, to protect her. The thing was, Sunwoo wasn't only doing it for her anymore, but it was for all of them. He knew what kind of information this person had, and how it could potentially destroy his entire operation, his entire business, his entire gang. They would all go to jail for life back in the States if word of their operations got out. He could not and would not let that happen, all of them had worked too hard to get thrown to the dirt. 

He would take him down before he took them down, and he swore on that. Whether he could figure out the hidden card or not, the big day would have to happen, and Sunwoo would make sure he was prepared for anything. 




+



"I love you guys," Solar had blurted, her green eyes raking over all the wonderful people she adored in her living room, "I'm really thankful to be united with all of you again."

Her words were out of place and sentimental, causing everyone to look to her, some with confusion, some with surprise, and some blank as if they just heard nothing at all. There, in the living room, was a crowd of people the average person wouldn't think would all be together. But there they were, a bunch of criminal gang members, preppy college students, and famous Korean idols, all brought together by something so dark. Yet, they had found friendships in places they wouldn't expect, and old friends and family had been reunited after what was nearly a decade. It was special. Solar was gleeful to have all these people she cared about so close to her, and she felt like she should tell them, because there was no telling what the next couple of days would bring. 

There was no telling if they would make it out alive. Sunwoo and the members could assure everyone it would be fine and they had it planned out, but you couldn't be so sure anymore. Too many things had already gone wrong. Solar couldn't allow herself to think this would go according to plan, it never did.

"We love you, too, Sol!" Kevin was the first to cheer, sounding as if he was chanting a fan chant like a weirdo. 

Chanhee had sighed as the weird boy had thrown his fists in the air, shaking his head, "God, you are embarrassing."

"I love you, of course, my dear Solar," Hyunjoon spoke up from his spot across the room, sending her a wink. 

She was sitting in between Sunwoo and Jinhwan, and the two had both sent different types of looks to Hyunjoon. Sunwoo was just blatantly disgusted, and Jinhwan was still confused about how someone like that could be such a sadistic criminal. 

"I mean it," she stated after a second, "I don't know how what's gonna happen on Friday, so I wanted to make sure you guys all knew that. It means a lot to me what you've all done for me."

"You deserve it all, Mi," Jungkook was beside Jinhwan, and he leaned forward to look at her across the couch, sending her that little smile of his. 

She returned it warmly. 

"You say that as if anything bad is going to happen," Sunwoo yawned, bored of the sappy conversation, "it'll be over and done, that's it."
